caveats: a: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
a: Standard motors, specialty motors, generator sets, and factory rewinding have different labor and engineering profiles.
rho: Standards and energy-use evidence establish constraints and value, not an observed implementation rate.
rho: Smaller custom manufacturers may lack standardized data and digital machines.
e: Eligibility depends on treating engineered-to-order and factory rebuild manufacturing as outsourced service.
e: The frozen count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE and may include product-only suppliers or firms outside the band.

s5: Some factory rebuild operations may be classified inconsistently with repair services.

q: Retention differs between commodity catalog motors, custom units, generators, and urgent factory rebuilds.
d5: Electricity demand is not a direct motor or generator shipment forecast.
d5: Imports, permanent-magnet supply, efficiency-driven downsizing, variable-speed drives, and economic cycles can change domestic demand.

o: Variable-speed drives and equipment integration can reduce some standalone motor demand without eliminating accountable rotating machinery.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Quoted per-unit, program, or project sales, often configured or engineered to OEM and industrial specifications, with design, materials, testing, documentation, warranty, repair, and lifecycle support bundled under repeat accounts.
lens.excluded_activities: Automotive starting motors and alternators, turbine generator sets, nonfactory repair shops, import-only distributors, captive plants, firms outside the EBITDA band, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly engineering, winding, assembling, testing, rebuilding on a factory basis, and supplying electric motors, power generators, and motor-generator sets to external OEM, industrial, commercial, utility, contractor, and distributor customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations across NAICS 335 rather than wage-weighted tasks in LMM motor and generator manufacturers. The range maps exposure to electromagnetic and mechanical design support, specification parsing, quoting, procurement, winding and test-data analytics, scheduling, quality documentation, predictive maintenance, and service triage against lower exposure in laminating, shaft and housing work, coil winding, insulation, balancing, assembly, machining, impregnation, testing, material handling, and rebuild work; broad data may understate specialized engineering and machining.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
Standard motors, specialty motors, generator sets, and factory rewinding have different labor and engineering profiles.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census across at least fifteen LMM motor, generator, and factory-rebuild plants, separating engineering, quoting, procurement, planning, lamination, winding, insulation, machining, assembly, balancing, impregnation, testing, quality, maintenance, logistics, repair, and service.
a.rationale: BLS reports 65,350 electrical and electronic assemblers, 57,660 team assemblers, and 5,790 coil winders across NAICS 335, showing a physical production core. AI can support specification-heavy design, planning, and diagnostics, but rotor, stator, winding, assembly, balancing, and load testing remain operator-intensive.

d5.bridge: DOE motor energy-use evidence and EIA electricity-demand growth proxy demand drivers rather than constant-quality motor and generator shipments to eligible LMM firms. Efficiency rules can increase value per unit while reducing energy use; electricity growth, data centers, industrial investment, backup generation, and electrification affect product segments differently, while imports may capture US demand.
caveats: Electricity demand is not a direct motor or generator shipment forecast.
Imports, permanent-magnet supply, efficiency-driven downsizing, variable-speed drives, and economic cycles can change domestic demand.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year unit and megawatt forecasts by industrial motor class, specialty motor, standby generator, distributed generation, OEM program, and factory rebuild, with industrial capital spending, data-center projects, efficiency-rule redesign, imports, installed-base age, backlog, and customer forecasts.
d5.rationale: DOE states motors drive about 54% of industrial electricity use, making them essential across pumps, fans, conveyors, compressors, and processes. EIA reports record 2025 generation up 2.8%, with commercial and industrial demand growth and further growth expected in 2026-2027. Electrification, automation, data centers, grid and backup capacity, efficiency upgrades, and installed-base replacement support modest five-year growth.

caveats: Eligibility depends on treating engineered-to-order and factory rebuild manufacturing as outsourced service.
The frozen count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE and may include product-only suppliers or firms outside the band.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level screen of the 234 estimated candidates covering normalized EBITDA, custom and repeat-program revenue, external versus affiliate sales, factory rebuild share, domestic value added, customer concentration, owner dependence, certifications, warranty and failure history, backlog, and plant condition.
e.rationale: Custom motors, generators, OEM programs, and factory rebuilding often function as recurring outsourced engineering and manufacturing services. Eligibility is reduced by commodity product sellers, strategic subsidiaries, captive shops, import-led operations, nonfactory repair, owner-held relationships, weak certifications, customer concentration, and businesses outside normalized EBITDA limits.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 335312 ($2,956,046,295) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 335312 ($14,789,755,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $508,589 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 335312); class <5: 113 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,310,575 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,434,335 -> IN $1-10M band; class 5-9: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,872,844 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $367,452 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,378,347 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,251,432 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 72 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,644,111 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,873,175 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 27 firms, avg revenue/firm $53,639,519 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,524,074 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 43 firms, avg revenue/firm $257,306,977 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $50,483,629 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 234

caveats: Manufacturing may consolidate into large global operators outside the lens.
Variable-speed drives and equipment integration can reduce some standalone motor demand without eliminating accountable rotating machinery.
o.change_evidence: OEM and industrial sourcing data on standardized imports, integrated direct-drive equipment, solid-state substitutes, additive and lights-out production, predictive maintenance, and repair-versus-replace, alongside certification and field-liability requirements.
o.rationale: Remaining demand requires an accountable operator to interpret specifications, design electromagnetic and mechanical systems, source traceable materials, manufacture and insulate windings, machine and balance rotating parts, assemble, test, document efficiency and safety, and stand behind field performance. Software can optimize and monitor but cannot supply the certified rotating equipment.

caveats: No representative contract or pass-through dataset was located.
Retention differs between commodity catalog motors, custom units, generators, and urgent factory rebuilds.
q.change_evidence: Customer- and product-family margin bridges before and after automation, including copper, steel and magnet costs, tender terms, annual cost-downs, expedited premiums, lead times, field failures, warranties, rebuild pricing, volumes, and resourcing.
q.rationale: Custom designs, certifications, installed-base knowledge, reliability, downtime avoidance, approved-vendor status, and repair turnaround support retention. OEM tenders, distributor negotiation, copper and steel escalators, efficiency standards, alternative sourcing, and annual cost-downs pass common gains through. Reliability and lead-time improvements should retain better than transparent reductions in labor content.

caveats: Standards and energy-use evidence establish constraints and value, not an observed implementation rate.
Smaller custom manufacturers may lack standardized data and digital machines.
rho.change_evidence: Two-year pre/post deployments for AI specification parsing, motor design, quoting, material substitution, winding planning, scheduling, vibration and test analytics, machine vision, predictive maintenance, documentation, and service, reporting labor, yield, failures, lead time, and overrides.
rho.rationale: CAD and electromagnetic models, bills of material, quotes, routings, winding records, machine data, vibration signatures, test curves, field telemetry, and warranty histories support AI in engineering, planning, inspection, maintenance, and service. Adoption is constrained by DOE efficiency and test rules, safety and certification, variable custom work, legacy machinery, traceability, cybersecurity, and severe consequences of field failure.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners across industries planned a sale or ownership transfer within five years. Discounting intentions for failed sales, internal transfers, closures, strategic subsidiaries, and asset-only deals yields the base; fragmented specialty and repair ownership may raise succession, while certifications, owner technical knowledge, customer concentration, and capital needs impede transfer.
caveats: Gallup is not industry- or size-specific and measures plans rather than completed transfers.
Some factory rebuild operations may be classified inconsistently with repair services.
s5.change_evidence: Five to ten years of NAICS 335312 closed deals, plant sales, closures, and owner changes from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, electrical-equipment brokers, EASA networks, SBA loans, UCC records, and state registries, matched to eligibility and owner age.
s5.rationale: The estimated population is large enough for recurring succession and strategic capacity deals, but qualifying going-concern transfers require preservation of skilled labor, approvals, engineering knowledge, customer programs, warranties, working capital, and equipment.

principal_driver: Essential installed-base equipment and electrification create recurring custom, replacement, and rebuild demand around data-rich engineering and test workflows.
principal_weakness: Physical production, certification, and OEM price pressure limit substitution and retained savings.
